Metadata.Coram: Andre Maniam J, Dominique Hascher IJ and Christopher Scott Sontchi IJ

Party_Details.Name: Baker, Michael A (executor of the estate of Chantal Burnison, deceased)

Facts.Text: Dr Chantal Burnison was the co-inventor of the compound Ethocyn.
BCS initiated legal proceedings against Baker and BCS Pharma corporation in California concerning the rights to Ethocyn.
The parties agreed to a stay of the Californian proceedings in June 2020.
The stay on the Californian proceedings was lifted in March 2021.
A court order was issued restraining the prosecution of specific categories of claims within the Californian proceedings.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the continued prosecution of prohibited claims in a foreign jurisdiction following a clear judicial restraint constitutes a breach of legal obligations and warrants the imposition of penalties.
Party_Details.Rule: Disobedience of a court order occurs where a party, having knowledge of the order, intentionally acts in a manner inconsistent with its terms. Such conduct is subject to financial penalties or other sanctions intended to protect the administration of justice.
Party_Details.Application: A party obtained a judicial restraint against the pursuit of certain claims in California. Despite the order, the opposing corporate party and its controlling individual continued to maintain those claims in their court filings. The controllers of the company were aware of the order but chose to proceed with the foreign litigation to protect interests they believed they were entitled to pursue.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court held that BCS and Marcus Weber committed civil contempt by intentionally disobeying the anti-suit injunction. The court found they had knowledge of the order and continued to prosecute the injuncted claims, even after the Court of Appeal expressly directed their withdrawal. BCS’s own filings acknowledged non-compliance, indicating that full compliance would require surrendering rights it wished to pursue. The court found several aggravating factors: the breach was deliberate and continuous over two years, and the contemnors took active steps in the foreign court to restrain the enforcement of the Singapore order. Despite these factors, the court decided against imprisonment because the foreign court had not yet granted relief undermining the Singapore judgment. The court also determined that civil contempt under the AJPA does not constitute a criminal conviction for the purposes of director disqualification under the Companies Act. Consequently, the court imposed significant fines on BCS and Weber but declined the disqualification order.

Metadata.Coram: Andre Maniam J, Dominique Hascher IJ and Christopher Scott Sontchi IJ

Party_Details.Name: BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd was managed by directors Hartono Sianto and Chuah Lay San.
BCS filed a Third Amended Complaint in the Californian proceedings which included claims that were the subject of a subsisting restraint order.
BCS filed a memorandum stating that full compliance with the Singapore judicial restraint would require it to surrender rights it wished to pursue in the United States.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a corporation is liable for penalties when it continues to prosecute foreign claims that have been expressly prohibited by a domestic court order.
Party_Details.Rule: A corporation is responsible for intentional disobedience of court orders when it continues restrained proceedings with full knowledge of the order's terms.
Party_Details.Application: BCS was the claimant in a Californian lawsuit and was specifically named in a domestic order restraining the pursuit of certain categories of those claims. Rather than withdrawing the claims, BCS filed an amended complaint retaining them and formally notified the court of its intention to continue the litigation despite the restraint.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court determined that BCS intentionally disobeyed the anti-suit injunction. Knowledge of the order was established through the company's prior legal challenges to the restraint. The court found that BCS's retention of the prohibited claims in its Third Amended Complaint and its subsequent memorandum stating that compliance would be detrimental to its interests constituted an explicit intention to remain in breach. Aggravating factors included the two-year duration of the breach and active attempts to block the enforcement of the domestic order in the foreign jurisdiction. The court imposed a fine of SGD 80,000 on the company.

Metadata.Coram: Andre Maniam J, Dominique Hascher IJ and Christopher Scott Sontchi IJ

Party_Details.Name: Marcus Weber

Facts.Text: Weber was the sole shareholder and director of BCS Business Consulting Services Pte Ltd.
Weber was personally named in a judicial order restraining the prosecution of certain foreign claims relating to the Ethocyn trust.
Weber provided instructions to United States legal counsel regarding the management of the Californian proceedings.
Weber assumed the role of sole director of BCS after the previous director suggested the withdrawal of the prohibited claims.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a controlling individual who directs a company to maintain prohibited foreign litigation is liable for personal penalties and subject to professional disqualification.
Party_Details.Rule: Individuals who procure a company to act in defiance of a court order commit civil contempt. Professional disqualification requires a criminal conviction of a specified class, which excludes findings of civil contempt for simple disobedience.
Party_Details.Application: Marcus Weber controlled the legal strategy for a company involved in a multi-jurisdictional dispute. When a domestic order prohibited the pursuit of specific claims, he authorized the company's US lawyers to continue the litigation. He subsequently removed a director who advocated for compliance with the order and took over the directorship himself to maintain the company's legal position.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court found that Marcus Weber personally directed the company's contemptuous conduct. Evidence showed he was the 'controlling mind' who instructed US counsel to retain the injuncted claims and specifically replaced the previous director to ensure the litigation continued. The court imposed the maximum statutory fine of SGD 100,000, noting that he was a Singapore resident and the breach was deliberate and sustained. However, the court dismissed the application for his disqualification as a director, ruling that a finding of civil contempt is not a criminal 'conviction' as defined under section 154 of the Companies Act.

Metadata.Coram: Andre Maniam J, Dominique Hascher IJ and Christopher Scott Sontchi IJ

Party_Details.Name: Renslade Holdings Ltd

Facts.Text: Renslade Holdings Ltd paid a specified sum into the court's custody in Singapore as part of an earlier judicial order.
Renslade was named as a party in an order restraining the prosecution of global proceedings relating to Ethocyn assets.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether an entity named in a judicial restraint order has engaged in conduct that warrants penalties.
Party_Details.Rule: Penalties for disobedience of court orders require evidence of specific acts taken by the entity that are inconsistent with the terms of the order.
Party_Details.Application: Renslade was named in a broad judicial order alongside other parties. While other parties in the group took active steps to maintain prohibited claims in California, the specific conduct of Renslade following the order did not include independent acts of litigation or instructions that violated the restraint.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court's analysis focused on the active conduct of BCS and Marcus Weber in California. While Renslade was a named party in the original injunction, the application for penalties did not establish that Renslade had taken separate post-injunction actions to prosecute the prohibited claims. Consequently, no findings of liability or penalties were made against this entity.
